title: Rheumatoid Arthritis
oneLiner: Chronic autoimmune inflammatory arthritis; symmetrically affects small joints; leads to joint destruction, disability & systemic complications.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cervical spine instability (atlantoaxial subluxation)** – *finding:* new neck pain + upper limb paraesthesia / weakness or Lhermitte sign; can cause acute cord compression and respiratory arrest.  
- **Septic arthritis in an inflamed joint** – *finding:* single hot, swollen, red joint with inability to weight-bear / passive movement pain; treat as septic until proven otherwise (Gram stain + culture).  
- **Acute rheumatoid vasculitis** – *finding:* digital infarcts, mononeuritis multiplex, palpable purpura; can progress to mesenteric ischaemia or mononeuritis.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms**  
  - Symmetrical pain and swelling in MCPs, PIPs, wrists, feet (MTPs)  
  - Morning stiffness lasting >30 minutes (>1 hour is classic)  
  - Fatigue, low-grade fever, malaise  
- **Key exam findings**  
  - Synovitis: boggy swelling, warmth, tenderness over joints  
  - Deformities: ulnar deviation, swan-neck/boutonnière fingers, Z-thumb  
  - Extra-articular signs: rheumatoid nodules (elbows, fingers), scleritis, sicca, pleural rub, interstitial lung crackles
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely**  
  - Psoriatic arthritis: skin/nail psoriasis, DIP involvement, dactylitis; *splitter:* psoriasis rash or nail pits differentiate.  
  - Osteoarthritis: no morning stiffness, Heberden’s/Bouchard’s nodes, DIP/IP involvement; *splitter:* X-ray shows osteophytes, not erosions.  
  - Gout: acute monoarthritis (podagra), tophi; *splitter:* urate crystals on joint aspiration.  
  - Systemic lupus erythematosus: rash, renal, serositis; *splitter:* anti-dsDNA, low C3/C4.  
  - Reactive arthritis: history of diarrhoea or urethritis, conjunctivitis, enthesitis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside**  
  - **Joint aspiration** (urgent) if single hot swollen joint → Gram stain, culture, crystals (exclude septic arthritis and gout).  
  - Consider ECG if pericarditis suspected (chest pain, ST elevation).  
- **Bloods**  
  - FBC: normocytic anaemia, thrombocytosis.  
  - ESR, CRP (elevated in active disease).  
  - RF (sensitive, not specific) and anti-CCP (high specificity for RA).  
  - LFTs, renal function, hepatitis B/C serology before starting DMARDs.  
- **Imaging**  
  - First-line: plain X-ray hands + feet (bilateral) – look for periarticular osteopenia, soft tissue swelling, marginal erosions.  
  - Second-line: ultrasound or MRI of hands/wrists to detect early synovitis/erosions.  
- **Diagnostic criteria**  
  - 2010 ACR/EULAR classification criteria – score points from joint involvement (0–5), serology (0–3), acute phase (0–1), symptom duration (0–1); ≥6/10 = definite RA.  
- **Never-miss test**  
  - **Urgent joint aspiration** of any hot swollen joint in a patient with known RA – septic arthritis can mimic a flare and kills the joint within hours.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**  
  - Exclude septic arthritis (aspiration).  
  - Pain control: paracetamol 1g QDS + NSAID (e.g. naproxen 500mg BD) if no contraindication; add opioid if severe.  
  - If suspected RA vasculitis or severe systemic flare: high-dose steroids (e.g. methylprednisolone 500mg–1g IV once) – but only after septic arthritis excluded.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h)**  
  - Start or escalate DMARDs – **methotrexate first-line** (see Drugs).  
  - Bridging corticosteroids: oral prednisolone 10–20mg daily until DMARD takes effect (usually 6–12 weeks).  
  - Monitor CRP, joint counts, function daily.  
- **Long-term / outpatient**  
  - DMARD monotherapy (MTX) → if inadequate response at 3–6 months, add another DMARD (e.g. sulfasalazine, leflunomide) or switch to biologic (e.g. adalimumab, etanercept).  
  - Maintain folic acid supplement if on MTX.  
  - Annual flu vaccine, pneumococcal vaccine; avoid live vaccines while on immunosuppression.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**  
  - Fluids: IV saline 0.9% 1L/8h if nil by mouth or dehydrated.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC od (if immobile, age >40 or high risk).  
  - Diet: normal unless post-operative.  
  - Observations: 4-hourly routine; if NEWS >5 escalate.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents**  
  - **Methotrexate** (MTX): oral/SC 7.5–20mg once weekly. **Folic acid** 5mg once weekly (day after MTX). *(Dose escalation per local guideline – start 7.5mg, increase by 2.5–5mg every 4–6 weeks up to 20mg.)*  
  - **NSAIDs**: naproxen 500mg BD oral; ibuprofen 400mg TDS PRN (for flare only, not long term).  
- **Common alternatives**  
  - Sulfasalazine: 500mg–1g BD (max 3g/day).  
  - Leflunomide: 10–20mg daily.  
  - Hydroxychloroquine: 200–400mg daily (mild disease).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions**  
  - MTX: avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding, significant liver disease, alcohol >14 units/week, renal impairment (eGFR <30). Check LFTs, renal, FBC before each dose escalation.  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30, active GI bleed, or on anticoagulation.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**  
  - MTX: reduce dose or avoid if eGFR <30; hold if AST/ALT >3x ULN.  
  - Leflunomide: avoid if significant liver impairment; dose adjust per LFTs.  
- **Most dangerous dose error**  
  - **Methotrexate prescribed daily instead of weekly** – causes marrow suppression, mucositis, fatal toxicity. Always check script: "Once weekly" written clearly.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute**  
  - Septic arthritis (50% mortality/morbidity if missed).  
  - Acute cervical myelopathy (neck injury/trauma or spontaneous subluxation).  
  - Drug reactions: MTX pneumonitis (dry cough, dyspnoea, fever), infection risk.  
- **Chronic**  
  - Joint erosions and deformities → disability.  
  - Cardiovascular disease (accelerated atherosclerosis).  
  - Amyloidosis (rare, nephrotic syndrome, hepatosplenomegaly).  
  - Osteoporosis (steroid use + inactivity).
sections.Prognosis: - Chronic progressive disease; 5–10% achieve long-term remission. Early DMARDs reduce erosions and improve function. Life expectancy reduced by 5–10 years, mainly due to CV disease.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always assess cervical spine** (flexion/extension XR) before any intubation or surgery in RA >10 years / with neck symptoms.  
- **Aspirate every hot swollen joint** – if monoarticular flare, don’t assume it’s a flare until septic arthritis ruled out.  
- **Start DMARD therapy early** – within 6 weeks of diagnosis to prevent joint damage.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give long-term steroids without DMARD cover** – causes osteoporosis, diabetes, and disease progression.  
- **Do not ignore infection** – RA patients on immunosuppression often present without fever; treat empirically if unwell.  
- **Do not prescribe MTX daily** – deadly error; always verify weekly dosing on drug chart.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New hot, swollen joint (septic arthritis).  
  - New neck pain, limb weakness, or difficulty walking (cervical cord compression).  
  - Fever, rigors, or breathlessness (infection or MTX pneumonitis).  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Rheumatology outpatient within 4–6 weeks for new diagnosis or disease flare.  
  - Blood monitoring: FBC, LFTs, renal function every 2 weeks until stable, then every 1–3 months.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/anaesthetics immediately for:**  
  - Suspected cervical cord compression (new neurological deficit, respiratory difficulty).  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L despite initial resuscitation.  
  - GCS <15 or falling consciousness.  
- **Other red flags:**  
  - Suspected septic arthritis – orthopaedic consult for washout and IV antibiotics.  
  - Acute dyspnoea with new infiltrates on CXR (MTX pneumonitis) – stop MTX, call respiratory.

rawMarkdown:
## Rheumatoid Arthritis  
**One-liner:** Chronic autoimmune inflammatory arthritis; symmetrically affects small joints; leads to joint destruction, disability & systemic complications.  

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?  
- **Cervical spine instability (atlantoaxial subluxation)** – *finding:* new neck pain + upper limb paraesthesia / weakness or Lhermitte sign; can cause acute cord compression and respiratory arrest.  
- **Septic arthritis in an inflamed joint** – *finding:* single hot, swollen, red joint with inability to weight-bear / passive movement pain; treat as septic until proven otherwise (Gram stain + culture).  
- **Acute rheumatoid vasculitis** – *finding:* digital infarcts, mononeuritis multiplex, palpable purpura; can progress to mesenteric ischaemia or mononeuritis.  

### Presentation  
- **Cardinal symptoms**  
  - Symmetrical pain and swelling in MCPs, PIPs, wrists, feet (MTPs)  
  - Morning stiffness lasting >30 minutes (>1 hour is classic)  
  - Fatigue, low-grade fever, malaise  
- **Key exam findings**  
  - Synovitis: boggy swelling, warmth, tenderness over joints  
  - Deformities: ulnar deviation, swan-neck/boutonnière fingers, Z-thumb  
  - Extra-articular signs: rheumatoid nodules (elbows, fingers), scleritis, sicca, pleural rub, interstitial lung crackles  

### Differential Diagnosis  
- **Most likely → less likely**  
  - Psoriatic arthritis: skin/nail psoriasis, DIP involvement, dactylitis; *splitter:* psoriasis rash or nail pits differentiate.  
  - Osteoarthritis: no morning stiffness, Heberden’s/Bouchard’s nodes, DIP/IP involvement; *splitter:* X-ray shows osteophytes, not erosions.  
  - Gout: acute monoarthritis (podagra), tophi; *splitter:* urate crystals on joint aspiration.  
  - Systemic lupus erythematosus: rash, renal, serositis; *splitter:* anti-dsDNA, low C3/C4.  
  - Reactive arthritis: history of diarrhoea or urethritis, conjunctivitis, enthesitis.  

### Investigations  
- **Bedside**  
  - **Joint aspiration** (urgent) if single hot swollen joint → Gram stain, culture, crystals (exclude septic arthritis and gout).  
  - Consider ECG if pericarditis suspected (chest pain, ST elevation).  
- **Bloods**  
  - FBC: normocytic anaemia, thrombocytosis.  
  - ESR, CRP (elevated in active disease).  
  - RF (sensitive, not specific) and anti-CCP (high specificity for RA).  
  - LFTs, renal function, hepatitis B/C serology before starting DMARDs.  
- **Imaging**  
  - First-line: plain X-ray hands + feet (bilateral) – look for periarticular osteopenia, soft tissue swelling, marginal erosions.  
  - Second-line: ultrasound or MRI of hands/wrists to detect early synovitis/erosions.  
- **Diagnostic criteria**  
  - 2010 ACR/EULAR classification criteria – score points from joint involvement (0–5), serology (0–3), acute phase (0–1), symptom duration (0–1); ≥6/10 = definite RA.  
- **Never-miss test**  
  - **Urgent joint aspiration** of any hot swollen joint in a patient with known RA – septic arthritis can mimic a flare and kills the joint within hours.  

### Management  
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**  
  - Exclude septic arthritis (aspiration).  
  - Pain control: paracetamol 1g QDS + NSAID (e.g. naproxen 500mg BD) if no contraindication; add opioid if severe.  
  - If suspected RA vasculitis or severe systemic flare: high-dose steroids (e.g. methylprednisolone 500mg–1g IV once) – but only after septic arthritis excluded.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h)**  
  - Start or escalate DMARDs – **methotrexate first-line** (see Drugs).  
  - Bridging corticosteroids: oral prednisolone 10–20mg daily until DMARD takes effect (usually 6–12 weeks).  
  - Monitor CRP, joint counts, function daily.  
- **Long-term / outpatient**  
  - DMARD monotherapy (MTX) → if inadequate response at 3–6 months, add another DMARD (e.g. sulfasalazine, leflunomide) or switch to biologic (e.g. adalimumab, etanercept).  
  - Maintain folic acid supplement if on MTX.  
  - Annual flu vaccine, pneumococcal vaccine; avoid live vaccines while on immunosuppression.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**  
  - Fluids: IV saline 0.9% 1L/8h if nil by mouth or dehydrated.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC od (if immobile, age >40 or high risk).  
  - Diet: normal unless post-operative.  
  - Observations: 4-hourly routine; if NEWS >5 escalate.  

### Drugs & Doses  
- **First-line agents**  
  - **Methotrexate** (MTX): oral/SC 7.5–20mg once weekly. **Folic acid** 5mg once weekly (day after MTX). *(Dose escalation per local guideline – start 7.5mg, increase by 2.5–5mg every 4–6 weeks up to 20mg.)*  
  - **NSAIDs**: naproxen 500mg BD oral; ibuprofen 400mg TDS PRN (for flare only, not long term).  
- **Common alternatives**  
  - Sulfasalazine: 500mg–1g BD (max 3g/day).  
  - Leflunomide: 10–20mg daily.  
  - Hydroxychloroquine: 200–400mg daily (mild disease).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions**  
  - MTX: avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding, significant liver disease, alcohol >14 units/week, renal impairment (eGFR <30). Check LFTs, renal, FBC before each dose escalation.  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30, active GI bleed, or on anticoagulation.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**  
  - MTX: reduce dose or avoid if eGFR <30; hold if AST/ALT >3x ULN.  
  - Leflunomide: avoid if significant liver impairment; dose adjust per LFTs.  
- **Most dangerous dose error**  
  - **Methotrexate prescribed daily instead of weekly** – causes marrow suppression, mucositis, fatal toxicity. Always check script: "Once weekly" written clearly.  

### Complications  
- **Acute**  
  - Septic arthritis (50% mortality/morbidity if missed).  
  - Acute cervical myelopathy (neck injury/trauma or spontaneous subluxation).  
  - Drug reactions: MTX pneumonitis (dry cough, dyspnoea, fever), infection risk.  
- **Chronic**  
  - Joint erosions and deformities → disability.  
  - Cardiovascular disease (accelerated atherosclerosis).  
  - Amyloidosis (rare, nephrotic syndrome, hepatosplenomegaly).  
  - Osteoporosis (steroid use + inactivity).  

### Prognosis  
- Chronic progressive disease; 5–10% achieve long-term remission. Early DMARDs reduce erosions and improve function. Life expectancy reduced by 5–10 years, mainly due to CV disease.  

### Pearls (positive actions)  
- **Always assess cervical spine** (flexion/extension XR) before any intubation or surgery in RA >10 years / with neck symptoms.  
- **Aspirate every hot swollen joint** – if monoarticular flare, don’t assume it’s a flare until septic arthritis ruled out.  
- **Start DMARD therapy early** – within 6 weeks of diagnosis to prevent joint damage.  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)  
- **Do not give long-term steroids without DMARD cover** – causes osteoporosis, diabetes, and disease progression.  
- **Do not ignore infection** – RA patients on immunosuppression often present without fever; treat empirically if unwell.  
- **Do not prescribe MTX daily** – deadly error; always verify weekly dosing on drug chart.  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)  
- **Return if:**  
  - New hot, swollen joint (septic arthritis).  
  - New neck pain, limb weakness, or difficulty walking (cervical cord compression).  
  - Fever, rigors, or breathlessness (infection or MTX pneumonitis).  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Rheumatology outpatient within 4–6 weeks for new diagnosis or disease flare.  
  - Blood monitoring: FBC, LFTs, renal function every 2 weeks until stable, then every 1–3 months.  

### Escalation Criteria  
- **Call senior/anaesthetics immediately for:**  
  - Suspected cervical cord compression (new neurological deficit, respiratory difficulty).  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L despite initial resuscitation.  
  - GCS <15 or falling consciousness.  
- **Other red flags:**  
  - Suspected septic arthritis – orthopaedic consult for washout and IV antibiotics.  
  - Acute dyspnoea with new infiltrates on CXR (MTX pneumonitis) – stop MTX, call respiratory.
